. COURRENT AFFAIRS.

The ““ Ha'penny Dreadful ”” and Princes’s Street are
sparring out at each other! What a pity one or both
do not pluck up courage and bash into the old anti-
Trish shufiler in Westmoreland Street.  The ‘‘ Iree-
man’” has put on a spurt recently, and it has had a ‘‘go”’
or two ab the ** Independent.” ~ The paper of William
Martin Murphy, the rebelly haberdasher, evidently has
felt, hurt, and so it gives ‘ back answers ”in its lead-
ing article of November 29th. We would like to see
the poor old ‘* Freeman ' far stronger than it is, and

by all the rules of the game, if it were reasonably
plucky and efficient it should be at least on top of the
anti-Irish *“ Irish Times.”” From every national point
of view we would like to see the ** Treeman’’ develop-
ing a bit of pluck and efficiency. But why does it not
tackle the ‘* Irish Times’’? The *‘ Irish Times " is

making heaps of money. Iven into its births, mar-
riages and deaths column the Papists pour their money.
The ** Freeman” will be despised so long as it is afraid
of Arnott. And who is Arnott that anyone should be
aftaid of him? The “ Freeman’’ evidently succeeded
in nettling the ‘ Ha’penny Dreadful,”’ for the latter
paper thus opens out on the orthodox organ :--

So the ‘‘ Freeman’s Journal,”” pretending to
cater solely for Nationalists, is more pro-Liberal
than the * Daily Chronicle,” practically the offi-
cial mouthpiece of Iinglish Liberalism. This ultra-
Liberal tendency is, perhaps, but natural. All
branches of its office have helped to swell the army
of Government officials, and perhaps further
favours are anticipated, all forms of subsidy are
welcome. Of course it is inspired only by the
loftiest and purest motives of patriotism and un-
selfishness ; it is actually willing to die—in the pay
of its country or the British Government. Pap
fed with British gold,”” to use a phrasc not un-
familiar to some of its past and, perhaps, present
writers, it is peculiarly qualified to give lectures
on nationality. All these adventitious aids will,
no doubt, be supplemented by the usual snowball
resolutions, despite the failure of these familiar
dodges in the past, and the well-worn hacks who
have hitherto so often rendered service in the same
cause may be again requisitioned in the hope of
bringing some badly-needed grist to the mill.

! Neither the ¢ Independent’’ nor
had the courage to come out ade-
atrocious Norway importation. TIf
both of them editorially *‘ kept

Oh, what a Pr
the ‘‘ Freeman’
quately against the

we llect rightly,
saying nothing’” when the Issential Trish fight was on:

both of them, but more particularly the ‘“ Indepen-
dent,”” are afraid to manfully face the bigotry against
Catholic situation; and both of them, though willing
to peck at each other, are afraid to tackle Arnott’s con-
ternptible but highly profit-making paper. In Union-
ist Belfast there are two strong Unionist papers and a
struggling Home Rule paper: that is rather a natural
sibuation. But in that part of Home Rule Ireland that
has Dublin as its daily paper centre, we have one large
profit-making Unionist and anti-Trish paper, whilst we
have two alleged Nationalist papers, one trying to geb
off the rocks, and the other—whose Nationalism, to say
the least, is much more alleged than that of the ‘‘Free-
man "’—probably a source of financial loss to Trolly
Pole Murphy, the rather unsuccessful haberdasher. If
the ** Ha'penny Dreadful ™’ and the ‘‘ Penny Deplor-
able” will get into each other’s wool, why not both of
them ab least combine to attacl the miserable ‘ Irish
Times,” edited by a ‘ Saved " Healy?

There will be lots of pictures—original pictures, nob
photographs—in next week’s issue—our Christmas
Number. Price 2d.

There was o great blow-out recently ab Virginia, Co.
Cavan, ‘A capable and popular police constable ™
was feted and there was a night of hospitality and ora-
tory. Policeman Goldrich has been transferred from
Virginia to Cavan. If the report in the Cavan ‘‘Anglo-
Saxon’’ can be believed, this policeman was not a bad
sort of fellow, and, unlike some other bobbies, did more
than polish his boots and crease his hair. He did a
lot for: the local regatta, and when the . problem of &
shortage of boats had to be faced, he solved it by build-

boats himself, which was something more useful
‘than playing the latest Tondon: jingle on a: concertina.
Well, he was: entertained: to dinner at a local hotel, no
less than a D.I. being in the chair. We liope the poor
bobby does not by tlis: time thinlk He is as big a man
as the English Prime Minister. The District Inspector
expressed. the’ pleasure it gave him to preside over a
gathering of so many men whose views on many as-
~ pects of life were so opposite, but who had but one view
in assembling there that night—to express their unani-
mous esteem of Constable Goldrick, the guest of the
evening. The elite of the district in the shape of
bobbies appear to have been present. A Mr. Iitz-
simons, J.P., proposed the health of the local R.I.C.,
and Bobby Phipps replied.  This bobby stated that
Head Constable Cowan had desired him fo express the
regret . which he felt at being absent through illness.
Policeman Savage also made a speech, and the D.I.
sang a song. Who would be an uneasy king in the
Balkans if he might be a bobby in Ireland ?

A correspondent from the County Limer gives us
a very bad account of Sunday drinking: I had to
turn back from a walk this Sunday evening, the road
being desecrated by drunken men. One was a boy
about seventeen years. T met a so-called respectable
man on my way back who told me he had to dodge the
publichouses, his traitor friends hailing him from in-
side. There is not the sligh trouble in getting drink
in the villages on Sunday. As a matter of fact there
is more drink sold that day than any other day in the
week. What are the police doing? (There are several
in every village.) Do they join in the illicit drinking
themselves ? Tt looks like it. What are the magis-
trates doing? - Are any of them God-fearing? I
need not relate the misery the women and children of
those drunkards endure. It is known to everybody.””

One of the cartoons next week will concern the medi-
cal practitioners—it will be enjoyed more by the public
than by the doctors.

‘We observe in a daily paper a list of names under the

following heading and paragraph :—
HIGH SHERIFFS FOR 1913.
ELIGIBLE CANDIDATES

The names of the following gentlemen have been
returned by the Judges of Assize as eligible. to serve the
Office of High Sheriff during the ensuing yenr:—
There is a list of names—three ir: each case, we think
—against each county. So far as we may judge by
names they nearly all appear to be of England’s Faith-
ful Garrison crowd and ‘‘ Saved.”” Amongst the three
names against County Dublin is that of Capt. Wade
Thompson, the Pervert Priests’ Protector. One of the
three names for Kerry is Daniel Charles O’Connell,
who, we presume, is an ‘‘ Idolater,”” and probably a
Unionist, :

The heading to some correspondence in ““ Alf Fox,”
of Monday, excited our curiosity. The heading was
“Dr. Cox and the Medical Profession.”” What’s the
Privy Councillor up to now? It was a correspondence
that took place between the Right' Elonourable orator
and Dr. Hayes, the Hon. Sec. of ‘‘ The Dublin County
Borough Local Mcdical Committee.” It  evi-
dently has to do with the dispute between
Sawbones and the Insurance . Act or Iriendly
Societies.  Sawbones is  out for the highest

increased our curiosity, as we take the cap to fit us, for
we wrote, an article about the doctors some time ago,
an article which we have reason ftio) believe was h]gh]y

Sawbones in Ingland under certain conditions. Now
Sawbones in England, we undcrstand-f\ve tz\k.e prac-
tically no interest in England, and we give our impres-
sion on this matter subject to correction—is a thorn in
the side of the Liberal Grovernment. The Home Rule
Bill malkes the Liberal Government of interest and con-
com to Treland. Here is a paragraph from the letter
from Dr. Hayes, Hon. Sec. to the D.C.B.L.M.C. to the
Right Hon. Sawbones:

Tirst, with regard to the extension of medical
benefits to.this country. The profession in Ireland
and in England have agreed to act loyally by cach
other in any arrangement with the Government
which might be arrived at, so that if medical bene-
fits be extended to Ireland they can be accepted
only upon the same conditions as obfain in Jng-
land.

So the Sawbones of Treland, according to this, are in
alliance with the Sawbones of England. ~ If the English
doctors’ trades union refuse th overnment’s terms,
the Irish doctors agree to perform a ‘‘ sympathetic
strike ""—at least that is our reading of it. But that is
only by the way.

We gather that our only Right Honourable Sawbones
said something that it was feared might be interpreted
as a slur on ‘‘the profession "’—that phrase ‘‘ the pro-
fession ”’ reminds us of another dignified section of the
body national, ‘‘ the trade.”” Dr. Hayes remarks:

Permit me to say that if this interpretation is
correct, and it is not the intention of those who
read this meaning into what you have said to mis-
represent you (your statement at the meeting was
accepted as a repudiation of the policy of the pro-
fession), that neither in Dublin nor elsewhere in
Treland have the profession acted in any but an
honourable and dignified munner, although they
have been subjected to much provocation through
letters appearing in the daily papers and comments
in one weekly journal.

‘, Ardilaun!

Our only Right Hon. Sawbones appears to have got
quite an electric shock from this letter. He com-
mences: “T received your letter this morning, and
hasten to answer it. 1 am amazed that such @ mis-

. construction should have been put upon my words. I

could not have thought it possible.”” This quarre] or
storm in a tea-cup between the Right Ion. Cox and his
fellow-Sawbones is rather uninteresting and largely un-
intelligible to us as we have not time to follow these
matters closely. But it amuses us to find that * the
profession ** have not acted ““ in any but «n honourable
and dignified manner,” notwithstanding the provoca-
tion of comments in+‘ one weekly paper.” Why should
S the profession *’ become dishonourable by way of re-
taliating on our comments?

There is a great deal too much cant and rant about
““ the profession,’’ and it will not go down in this
country at the present time. Leaving open the ques-
tion whether doctors kill more than they cure, we are
certain that the public of the present day in Ireland
are sick of the cant and raimeis that is often talked
about this ‘‘noble profession ;" and whether it is nobie
or not depends very largely on particular facts. Tvery
honest trade is noble when the tradesman does his
duty. It is widely alleged and believed that many
candidates for dispensaries buy, or attempt to buy, the
votes of local public representatives who have the
giving away of such jobs. Why don’t ““ the profes-
sion ** tackle that state of affa If the doctors in
Treland are out for reform, if they look amongst their
own ranks they might find plenty to occupy them for
a good spell. If ““the profession ' have received pro-
vocation from our comments, let *‘ the profession *’
Indeed, we may say
that we are treating them to a cartoon next week in
our Christmas Number.

How many doctors know much, and how much do
young doctors know? Why are there so many doctors
in Ireland? Ts it because they are all consumed with
a passion to cure maladies and alleviate human suffer-
ing, or is it because the shop and the farm, industry
and commerce are held to be ‘‘ no class,”” and that it
'is ‘ genteel ’ to be a doctor ? The doctors had better
remember their place; for this country, green and tame
though it partly is, will not take too much lying down
from them.

‘“The Profession’’ and ‘‘the Trade’’ are not the
only things concerning which a lot of rant and cant is
indulged. ‘‘ The Press '’ is perhaps worse, for ‘‘ the
Press ” is able to boom itself through its own medium.
In the column next to the Sawbones’ letters there is
over half a: column report of the proceedings at a
dinner to the ex-editor of the evening paper run from
the office of the anti-Irish and anti-Catholic obscure
daily paper, Ardilaun’s * Bxpress.”” The ex-editor of
““ Bufi "'—an anti-Nationalist and anti-Irish paper,
whose policy was to rake in the ha’pennies of all creeds,
all classes—having got some job in England resigned
from ‘“ Buff.”” The man in question, a Mr. McPeake,
is a Catholic, and probably, if anything, a Nationalist
in politics; vet he edited * Buff.” Certainly there
was nothing exceptionally noble or uncoinpromising in
that.  All creeds, all classes went to the dinner in
A Mr.
J. E. Healy referred to “‘ Buff '’ as ‘‘a great news-
paper.’” McPeake, in replying, referred to “_Lhc sup-
port of a generous and public-spirited proprietor "'—
With the fulsome flattery, usual we sup-

pose on such occasions, he referred to Dublin as th_e
finest journalistic nursery in the world.”” Dublin is
such a wonderful journalistic nursery that Ireland is
the dumping ground of all sorts of journalistic pro-
ductions from BEngland! One Dublin evening paper
takes jokes daily from an Iinglish evening paper,
and another takes its cartoon from a second English
ovening paper.  ‘‘The Press,” “ The Profession,’’
“ The Trade ' are a pretty trio. We may say that a
series of interesting cartoons on “ The Press’’ will
appear in our next issue.

A very interesting feature of next Sunday’s Orches-
tral Concert will be the performance, for the first time,
of a new ‘Trish Fantasy,” for violin and piano, by
Hamilton Harty, the well-known Irish composer. The-
Tantasy comprises three numbers:—** The Mountain’s
High ”; a Reel by Hamilton Harty; and a Jig, ‘“ The
Burnt Ould Man.” Those who were so fortunate as
to be present a few Sundays back, when some extracts
from Harty’s music were played, will be keenly anti-
cipatory of the performance of the ‘‘ 'antasy " next
Sunday. The eminent Irish violinist, Mr. P. J. Griffith,
for whom this work has been specially written, will
surely give a masterly rendering of the work. As there
is sure to be a large audience at the Antient Concert
Rooms next Sunday, those intending to be present
should be in good time.

A series of cartoons on ‘‘ The Press,”” as we know it
partly in Ireland, will be an interesting feature of next
week’s Christmas Number. Price 2d.

We are becoming great Imperialists entirely these
days. Here is a paragraph from the *‘ truly National "
‘“‘ Treeman *’ of Saturday:—** Cinematograph Films.—
The South Australian Government has just sent to
England a number of cinematograph flms depicting
life in that country, and including a picture of a review
of cadets and of a test match. In any case such films
would be a weleome change from the surfeit of present-
ments of cowboy dramas and scenes from the American
Civil War, while their Imperial nature makes them of
real value.” So ‘‘ their Imperial nature *’ makes these
South Australian baits for emigrants of real value!
We suppcse in due time these films will do duty in
the picture houses that have sprung up in this country
more plentifully than even rinks sprang up a few years

~ The Bandon Banjo and Mandoline Orchestral Society

held another concert recently under the conductorship
of Mr. Bandon Birell. The affair opened with the
““Darkies’ Patrol,” and the next item was ‘* Little
Country Mice *’—rather appropriate for the Bandon B.
and M. O. S. A local bank clerk and a local pawn-
broker sang ‘‘ The Upper Ten and the Lower Five™;
we suppose the bank clerk is one of the upper ten of
Bandon. There was no song in Irish at this concert:
the vulgar tongue might hurt the tender susceptibilities
of the Bandon Birrellites. At the last meeting of the
Bandon Town Commissioners a member of that august
body referred to the notice that Bandon was receiving
from the LEADER recently. We understand that this
Town Commission is rather a laughing-stock even in
Bandon, and we may have something to suy about it
later on.

A correspondent draws our attention to the following
advertisement from a Belfast paper:—'* Wanted, Man,
25 to 85, for our Windsor Chair Department; must be
good benchman; some experienca of polishing; piece-
worker; strictly sober and industrio Protestant.—
James Sheriff and Son, Windsor Chair Factory, Coote-
hill, Co. Cavan.” We suppose if in ‘‘ the South and
West’’ a shopkeeper objected to a Cootehill Windsor
chair he would be regarded as bigoted !

Boers or South Africans at Lansdowne Road last week
—38 points to nothing. It is humiliating to have these
Rugby chaps masquerading before the sporting world
as an Irish team. Ireland only plays Rugby to a par-
tial extent; that Ireland which drew 20,000 people to
witness the great hurling match a few weeks ago is the
Treland of the athletes and that has nothing to do with

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 7, 1912,

Rugby. It is said that about 10,000 people were at
this match on Saturday at- which the South Afvican

men wiped the ground with the West Britons.  Cer-

tainly all sorts, shapes and sizes of Johnnies were there,
for on our way home we saw them going, and as we
don’t live far away, we strolled down to have a look af
them coming out. 1t used to be socks,'but now v.a
think we perceive a shifting of the Johnnies’ centre of
gravity, if we may put it that way, to the mufler. Al
Sorts of mufflers surrounded necks topped by every
variety of Johnny skull. Some Johnnies took cars, or
“‘caws,’”’ but there were a vast number of cars without
fares after the wiping of the ground process: we sup-
pose the Johunies had no spirit to take cars nftgr 38 to
nothing. It is a crying shame that the \vest Britons
who got such a drubbing should masquerade before the
public as an Irish team.

Mr. Bung and Mr. Bobby will not be ignored by our
artist next week.

Mr. Michael Breen, of the Bantry Band, is not quite
satisfied yet. He sends us a far longer letter than his
epistle of last week. . The All-made-in-England band of
Bantry is more important to Bantry than it is to Ire-
land, and the LEapEr is not a Bantry local paper. The
instruments for a brass band can be made in Ireland,
and the brass instruments—we have nothing to say
to reed instruments—for the Bantry band could have
been procured of Irish make. The establishing of «
new industry in a country is a grafting process more oi
less, and the firm that we referred to has made the
attempt to establish the brass band manufacturing in-
dustry in Dublin. The chief man in the mdustry in
Dublin is an Englishman settled in Dublin for many
years. Is that what Bantry is jibbing at? Does Ban-
try expect that brass band making will spring up like
a mushroom?  The Irish Cutlery Company, for in-
stance, have had to induce a few Shefiield or other
skilled workers to come here else the cutlery industry
could not have been established in Dublin.  How is
> brass band making industry to be established un-
ome outsider comes over to help to establish and

sain the skill in Treland 2 Mr. Sims is an English-
man, and he is having trained, or has trained, Irish-
men in the brass band making industry. 'The Bantry
Band could have had their brass instruments made in
Ireland, but they imported theirs from Iingland. M.
Breen, of the Bantry Band, several times insinuates
that we have a brief for the Irish band manufacturer.

- We certainly have, or rather assume, a brief for all

Irish manufacturers, though some of the manufacturers
have not the manners to say even ** thank you.” If
we have a brief for the Irish brass band industry we
can assure the All-made-in-England Brass Band of
Bantry that it is an unfee-ed brief—we don’t suppose
that is the kind of brief that Tim of the other and more
famous Bantry Band would appreciate. Mr. Breen,
of the Bantry Band, says we are incorrect in our sur-
mise that the Irish made brass instruments could
have been supplied cheaper than the foreign instru-
ments. Well, let him send us the two estimates and
we can decide the point quickly enough.

A scene enacted last Saturday on the quay of Ballin-
robe might have led to riot. About half a dozen men,
including a couple of water bailiffs, assembled on the
quay at three o’clock. The bailiffs carried a bag of
fishing nets, evidently captured on the Robe and on
some of the lakes. They piled them up on a large stone
pillar and set fire to them. A Ballinrobe reader re-
marks that nobody would object to their destroying
those nets, if they did so in a proper place, and at a
proper time; but making a great public event of it and
to show the rights and privileges that yet appertain to
the local ascendancy was almost calculated to make a
riot.  Where were the police that they allowed this
handful of people, all ““ Saved,”” we believe, to behave
in this manner ? ]

A Westport correspondent writes :—*‘ Recently the
Rev. Fr. Angelus, of the Capuchin Order, delivered a
lecture on Temperance in the Town Hall here, at which
the Rev. Fr. Canavan, Adm., presided, and during the
course of the lecture the rev. gentleman appealed to
the young men of the town to play the national games
of Treland and to be no longer aping the foreigner.
Notwithstanding this, a small section in the town
formed a Soccer team, and on Friday last they jour-
neyed all the way to Irvinestown, Co. Fermanagh, to
play a match there. The team’s arrival here on Satur-
day night at 11.30 was heralded by the music of a few
melodeons and three or four torches, when a procession
was formed at the station, which was headed by an
imported English fruiterer, and this imported individual
thought well to address them before they separated.
I think you will agree that matters have come to a
pretty pass when this English fruiterer was the only
spokesman among the ‘ natives.” ’’

The Dean’s Grange Cemetery is almost wholly a
Catholic cemetery, the number of burials being about
12 Catholics to 1 Protestant. It is governed by a Joint
Burial Board of 19 members, elected by Blackrock,
Kingstown, Dalkey, Killiney and Rathdown Rural (No.
1) District Council. The Board at present contains 13
Catholics and 6 non-Catholics. Since the formation of
the Board this practically Catholic cemetery has been
managed by Protestant officials, the only Catholics, so
far as we are aware, being a few grave diggers. Mr.
Burnell is registrar and secretary, and his daughter was
his paid assistant until she died some months ago.
Some time back, when the Nationalists were in a majo-
rity, the job of assistant was discontinued on the under-
standing that Mr. Burnell would do all the work. In
due time, when the Unionists got a majority, the post
was re-created, and Mr. Burnell’s daughter was ap-
pointed. At the present time the Protestant candi-
date for the position is Mrs. Burnell, wife of the pre-
sent Registrar. This Mr. Burnell is a monumental
sculptor in a large way, and apart from any other con-
sideration, it seems rather curious that a trader in
tombstones should be registrar of a public cemetery.
It is felt that at least the assistant to the registrar,
now that the position is vacant, should be held by a
Catholic. There have been some rather stormy meet-
ings held over the filling of this position, and it will
come along again at the next meeting. We will have
more to say about it later on.  Under the circum-
stances it is curious to find several Catholics using
their vote and influence to prevent even the minor
position of assistant to the Registrar to this almost
wholly Catholic cemetery being held by a Catholic. By
the way, besides being a monumental sculptor, the
Registrar is also in the milk selling line, though whe-
ther he is a registered dairyman or not we leave the
local authority to answer.

long way oft Captain Craig’s *‘ between two-thirds or

is no more superior to the rest of Ireland owing to Gal-
laher's Gold Plate, than Nottingham is superior to the
rest of England as a result of Player’s Navy Cut, or
Bristol from ‘* Wild Woodbines.’"

The whole argument is ridiculously absurd. Customs
duties, whether on tea, sugar, or tobacco, are no more
paid by the cities in which they are collected, than
fxcise duties ave paid by the cities containing brew-
eries and distilleries. Not only are the duties not paid
by the importers and manufacturers, but they are
passed on to the consumers, profits being made on the
duties as well as on the short prices. That this latter
fact is so is one of the leading arguments put forward
by all economists who object to indirect taxation, and
who maintain that the public pay a good deal more
an the Government get, or than the Government
ould exact if the public realised what they were pay-
ing. In the case of unmanufactured tobacco the dis-
proportion between the value of the article and the duty

THE CORRUPTION OF IRISH PLACE-NAMES.

T the last meeting of the Coiste Gnotha the
following resolution was passedi:—

“ That we demand from: the Ordnance: Survey De-
partment that they cease the practice of Anglicising
“the place-names of Ireland, and that the least we
demand is that the correct name in Irish be inserted
in each map and in each book.”

The forces in Ireland which are mainly responsible
for the corruption of our place-names are the Ordnance
Survey, the Post Office, the National Board of Educa-
tion, the Railways, and the authors of Popular Guides.

Let me take a few examples from the pavish in which
I was born, Cnocén (Knockane), Co. Kerry.

The Geadach is a branch of the Leamhain (corrupted
into Laune). On the Geadach are two bridges known
to everyone as Geadach Bridge (Droichead na
Geadaighe) and Lower Geadach Bridge (Droichead
Tochtrach na Geadaighe).  The lower bridge joins two
townlands, Whitefield (Bdn Chluain) and Tullig. It is
named on the Ordnance map as Gortnaskarry Bridge,
although the townland of Gortnaskarry (Gort na
Scairbhighe) is a mile away, and takes its name from
a shallow ford on the Leamhain, a ford which is com-
monly used by many people in the parish of Tuath as
a short cut to Milltown, the nearest bridge being at Kil-
lorglin, two or three miles further down. There are
two adjoining townlands in this parish of Tuath or
Cnocédn, Killoughane and Dromaloughane.  Anyone
looking at the ordnance map would conclude that both
were derived from the same root. But no. One is
Cill Léchdin, St. Léchan’s Church, and the other
Drom-a’ Lochain, the ridge of the pond. The pond
was there up to half a century ago, when it was drained
by Sir Rowland Blennerhassett. Of St. Ldchdin’s
Church only a part of the wall now stands near an old
limekiln on the roadside close to Tadhg Gabha’s forge.
I take it that part of the old walls had been pulled
down to build the kiln. Within a gunshot of the site
of this old church is ‘“ Castle Corr.””  Who would recog-
nise in this name, as Caislean an Chorruigh, the castle
of the marsh, the said marsh having been drained years
ago.

“0ld  sub-denominations  are disappearing  daily.
‘Cooleanig, now on the ordnance map and ofticial list
of townlands, includes Scairt an Cnoc, Lios Caillighe,
and Péirc Gharbh, but these latter have all disappeared.
Not far from there is the townland of Broolkhill on the
borders of the parish of Killorglin. I knew it as Caol
na bhFuardn, the brook of the springs. Brookhill now
includes this and Lisin-an-Fhearainn, the two most ex-
pressive Irish names having been blotted out for an
English one, which has no meaning. The next town-
land everyone knew and still knows as Cnocdn Déith.
‘Some upstart built a new slate house there some forty
years ago, which he called Mount David, and Mount
David is now the townland name on the map.

The townland of Coill (wood) is given Kill on the map.

Kilgobnet (Cill Ghobnatan) was at one time a well
Iknown spot in Kerry for its great fair, called *‘the
Pattern,”” on the feast of St. Gobnait, 11th Tebruary.

crana is the little junction on the railway called Tooban

. FATHER MATHEW UNION, LONDON.
(New Temperance Scheme.)

OME short time ago a notable society was formed

in London, viz., the Iather Mathew Union,

the membership of which is confined absolutely to total-
abstaining priests. We are x\ll)t to feel proud and grow
eloquent when we contemplate the proud position
which our Race has attained in all parts of the globe,
but there is unfortunately another side to this picture.
They have filled and they fill proud positions all over
the world, they have risen to power and influence
against desperate odds, but none but an Irish mis-
sionary priest can know to what depths of degradation
too many of our exiled countrymen have fallen. In the
fetid slums of the English and American cities you will
unfailingly meet them, wrecks of the finest spiritual
race on earth. We feel infinitely sad when we dwell
on all they or their immediate progenitors have left
behind, the pure air of an Irish valley or hillside, the
calm, restful life that glides on from a spotless youth
to an innocent old age, and the peaceful rest at last in
some quiet country churchyard among the bones of
their kindred. We may speak of progress—it is a great
word and beloved of economists—but the progress that
takes a man from his pure mountain home, however
humble, and lands him in a city slum, is a very doubt-
ful progression. How came he here? Surely not in
a thousand years did he inherit one instinct that would
impel him to select this environment as his home. I
could imagine him not out of place in a deserv or in
the broad prairie, in some howling waste where savage
beasts and venomous things have their dwelling-place.

. But in this reeking slum! O child of Patrick, it was

never. made for thee, and pity it is that any malign
destiny should ever have guided thy footsteps thither.
The children of Ireland are there, however, not in their
hundreds, but in their tens of thousands, and it is the
business of the missionary priest to keep track of them,
to watch over his flock in their grim and deadly sur-
roundings, to rouse to life a faith that, if sometimes
dormant, is never wholly dead. But above all is it
his business to get at the root of the evil, and that root
in ninety-nine cases out of a hundred is—Intemperance.
Effectually grapple with that curse, and you have
enormously raised the status of our Race abroad.
Knowing and feeling deeply all these things, the mem-
bers of the Father Mathew Union (the majority of
whom are Irish as the majority of their flocks are
Trigh), have dedicated themselves in a noble spirit of
self-sacrifice to the cause of Temperance; and short as
is their career bave gathered many under their banner,
and are doing noble and effective work.

The second annual meeting was held on November
6th, at the Presbytery, Red Cross St., The Borough,
London, S.E., Monsignor Doubleday presiding.
Among other important matters was discussed at con-
siderable length®the scheme to establish a National

62,”” and taking his rifle by the muzzle, he holds it at

Montenegro at Easter time is truly idyllic and beau-
titul. The people meet each other with the salutation,
*“ Christ is risen; Christ is risen indeed.”” In the best
room of every house the table is covered with abun-

stream go in and out. Iivery one is dressed in their
best; every one is happy, for the week before is one of

Such are these people, simple and ‘rnorul,' deeply re:
ligious in their domestic lives, but living a life of terror
and danger outside with the rifle or revo! never out
of the reach of their hands, for they .ow not the
moment when a Turk or an Albanian urged by the

property or lives or their women’s honour. But they
have held their own for centuries and now the blessed
day of their freedom has dawned at last.  Through
much blood they are reaching the goal, and please God
their future lives, the lives of those brave sons and
daughters of Montenegro will be peaceful, happy and
prosperous. May God grant it so. A people that pre-

odds were not destined to be ‘slaves.  They never
bowed the knee to man. They never submitted to the
Turk. They were the freest people in Europe and they
will remain so, free, independent, self-reliant and the

‘DO THEY GROW MEN DOWN YOUR WAY?"

THE question is Walt Whitman’s, and it is addressed

to Americans. But it is worth our while, as
Tvishmen, to address the query to ourselves. It will
do no harm if readers of this, the most thoughtful of
Trish journals, ask themselves the question:—Do they
grow men down your way? The query one that
should stimulate thought; it should give rise to fruitful
and profitable thinking. It should set people examin-
ing their consciences in regard to many excellent things
which they could quite easily do, and which they fre-
quently leave undone.

Ask yourself the question. gentle reader—don’t be
afraid ; we shall not probe too deeply—are you and your
friends acting in a way that befits sterling Catholics
eand patriotic Irishmen? No matter where your local
habitation may be, you can do useful work in the cause
of Taith and Fatherland. Are you, at this moment,
rendering the services which you might easily render to
your religion and your native land? If so, you can
calmly abide the issue of the question:—Do they grow
men down your way ?

As a test of your manhood, take the social work
which you may accomplish, if you have the good will
and energy to undertake it. You see poverty and dis-
4ress around you—what are you doing on behalf of the
poor and distressed? If you are, for instance, an
active member of the St. Vincent de Paul Society, you
are doing a good man’s work in one way.

But you can scarcely fail to recognise the fact that
poverty and distress are due, in large measure, to, the
deplorably low wages which are paid to so many classes
of Irish workers. Are you doing your best to advocate
that every wage-earner should receive—as Leo XIII.
has taught us the workers ought to receive—a living
wage? (By a “‘living wage’’ I mean such a wage as
shall enable the workingman and his family to live in
tolerable decency and comfort.) If you are yourself an
‘employer, do you pay “your employees a fair day’s
wage for a fair day’s work? Do you realise, and cause
others to realise, that working girls cannot live decently
on 3s. 6d., or 7s. 6d., or even 10s. 6d. per week? Do

THE LEADER. v ey

yow clearly perceive, and cause others to perceive, the
injustice of expecting a working man to rear his family

-on a wretiched wage of 14s. or 15s. a week?

Hitherto the farmers have been the curled darlings
of Trish legislation ; this was doubtless necessary, as the
farmers ave, in a sense, the backbone of the country.
But the workers, the wage-earners, have been terribly
neglected ; it is time that a strong and sustained effort
should be made to ameliorate their condition. At the
same fime, the effort must be a judicious and moderate
one; we must not overtax our struggling. industries.
But it is idle to hope for thriftiness among our working
people ; it is idle to hope for a cessation of the hideous
ovils that infest the tenements of our large cities; it is
idle to hope for the aholition of the damnable prosely- -
tism which the Birds’ Nest mercenaries carry on—so
long as hundreds of thousands of our workers are
miserably under-paid. ;

can shield them, from the temptations of the public-
house.  Hence we find that Catholic workingmen in
Australia rarely, if ever, sink to such a depth of degra-
dation as would render their children liable to become
the prey of Birds’ Nest fiends.

well organised.  Still, on the whole, the fact is un-
deniable that the great army of unskilled wage-earners,
to whose ranks we must add tens of thousands of girl-
workers, are deplorably under-paid.

Now, if the employers of skilled labour can afford to
pay (because they are compelled to pay) really good
wages to their workmen, is there any valid reason why
the employers of unskilled labour should not give their
a living wage? If builders and contractors

can afford to pay adequate wages, why should not

wealthy drapers, cenfectioners, and manufacturers do
the same? Of course, this does not mean that the un-
skilled worker should receive as much pay as his skilful
conirade. It does not mean that girl-workers should
be paid as highly as their fathers and brothers. But it
does mean that employers are not justified in taking
undue advantage of the feeble, the necessitous, the
disorganised, and the unskilful. It does mean that
employers should act conscientiously towards the least
and lowliest of their workérs, by paying them a decent
living* wage. It means, finally, that the unskilled
workers should band together, and organise their forces

_+ thoroughly, in order to bring pressure to bear upon
their employers, with a view to obtaining decent
remuneration for their services.

Low wages lead to chronic poverty; and poverty
compels multitudes of our Catholic working people to
dwell in foul tenements or insanitary hovels. — Such
dwellings are breeding-grounds of disease; they are
fatal to the physical well-being, and oftentimes to the
morals, of the inmates. How can a hapless father rear
up a healthy family in a squalid room or two of a filthy
Dublin tenement? In these grimy and congested

~ habitations, the children learn evil from their com-
panions; the adults, in many cases, receive the worst
of bad example from pheir neighbours.. Mothers of
families slip insensibly into careless, slatternly ways;
there is little” of comfort, or cleanliness, or privacy
about the home—if a domicile in such horrid surround-
ings could anywise merit the name of home. The

husband naturally succumbs to the superior attractions
of the publichouse. Too often the wife follows his
example ; she become: frequenter of the devastating
drink-shop. Little by little, the family sinks into direr
poverty and deeper degradation.  In time the parents
become drink-sodden wrecks, and the proselytising
fiend steps in and snatches the children.

It has been stated, on good authority, that fully a
thousand Catholic children are perverted, year by year,
in the Birds’ Nests of Dublin alone. If this be correct,

it discloses an appalling and most shameful state of
affaivs. It is imperative that Catholics should strain

¢évery nerve to prevent a continuance of this gigantic
evil. It is essential that Catholics should leave no
means untried to prevent Catholic children from being
taken to these homes of bigotry and falsehood. It is
right and neccssary, too, that Protestants should be
shamed into abandoning this hateful traffic,

I venture to think, however, that in this instance the
radical remedy is the only adequate remedy. Abolish
the cause of the evil, and the evil itself will speedily
‘disappear. If we desire to be as free from the baneful
cftects of proselytism as the Australian Catholics are,
we must see to it that our working people are, like the
Australian workers, properly paid, decently housed,
and shielded, as far as may be, from the pernicious
allurements of the drink-shop. It is abundantly clear
that these four things—low wages, tenement houses,
drink, and proselytism—are inseparably linked
together; they go hand in hand.

If you, good reader, are minded to do a manly man’s
part on behalf of this land of ours; if you are nobly
desirous of improving the moral and social condition of
our Catholic people; if you wish to do admirable work
for God and country, throw all your weight into the
scale on behalf of these four things:—

(1) Propes Wages for the Workers.
(2) Decent Housing for the People.
(8) The Prevention of Intemperance.
(4) The Aboliticn of Proselytism.

CHRISTMAS.

Again, at the approach of Christmas time, we ven--
ture to appeal to good and generous hearts for the poor
who are relieved at the Convent, or visited by our
Sisters.  We fear wearying people with frequent
appeals, but what can we do? The poor we have
always with us. They are God’s abiding gift to us,.
and must be cared. Each poor one is a poor Christ
for us.

If only they, whose homes are bright and happy,-
knew the want and suffering there is quite near them,
the good things they have would not be so pleasant
til] they had shared them in loving kindness with the
poor, Just now the shop windows are glittering with
thousands of beautiful and tempting things, One is
puzzled which to buy for child or friend or self. Wili:
you make a present to the poor? Many old things you:
put away as useless would be such a boon in their-
wretched homes. 0ld clothes, bits of carpet, bed
covering, books, ete., would be delightful presents to
them. When one is really hungry, ov very cold, or:
scantily clothed, a little food or fuel or clothing is so:
acceptable. Gret your little child to make up a bundle.
Accustom the little hand to give to God's Poor.

So many poor children come hungry to school thab
we now give breakfast to about 200 of them. These
have also to be clothed, and numbers of very poor
families are daily relieved at our Poor Kitchen. Per-
haps some may think we undertalke too much. Well,
it they knew the suffering that goes with great poverty,
and if they knew, as we know, the shocking tempta-
tions of many kinds by which these poor people are:
tempted, then they would not blame us for under-.
taking too much. ~Rather would they tell us not to.
spare ourselves, and even to risk somewhat in their
cause. How can anyone think much of toil or trouble
in face of poverty and pain and sinful temptation.

We sincerely trust God may put it into the hearts of
those to whom He has been very kind, to share God's.
good things with the suffering poor this Xmas time.
